chosen_title: Gifting cash to your wife to invest — the gift is free, the income isn't
caption:
You transfer ₹5 lakh to your wife's account. She opens an FD. The interest arrives — taxed in YOUR name. 😶

This is Section 64 of the Income Tax Act: the 'clubbing of income' rule. When you gift money or assets to your spouse, any income she earns by investing that gift is added straight back to YOUR taxable income, at YOUR slab rate. The gift travels tax-free. The profit does not.

*Illustration:* Husband in the 30% slab gifts ₹5 lakh. Wife parks it in an FD at 7%. She earns ₹35,000 interest. Clubbed back to husband — he pays ₹10,500 in tax he thought he'd escaped. Tax saved: ₹0. (Illustration only — actual tax depends on your full income.)

There ARE legal ways to split income in a family: gifts from her own parents or relatives, a genuine salary she draws from your business, adult children (18+) investing their own earnings, or an HUF. Each has its own rules and limits — a short conversation can tell you which fits.

What to do: Check every FD, mutual fund, or share account held in your spouse's name. If the seed money came from you, that income may already be clubbing into your ITR — and the IT department's AIS now flags mismatches automatically.

Drop us a WhatsApp — we'll untangle it in minutes.

Note: general information, not professional advice — talk to us for your specific case.
Source: Section 64, Income Tax Act, 1961.

slides.label: MONEY MYTH
slides.image_prompt: A middle-aged Indian husband handing a thick envelope of cash to his wife at a sunlit kitchen table, an FD receipt and a smartphone showing a bank balance lying between them, warm late-afternoon domestic glow, no text or numbers visible in the scene
slides.headline: You gifted ₹5L to your wife. The tax bill still lands on YOU.

slides.label: MYTH vs FACT
slides.myth: I gifted money to my wife — the income she earns from it is hers, so I pay less tax.
slides.truth: Section 64 'clubs' any income she earns from your gifted money straight back into YOUR taxable income, at YOUR slab rate. Same tax. Zero saving.

slides.label: REAL TALK
slides.headline: The plan looked smart. Section 64 quietly waited.

slides.label: THE NUMBERS
slides.body: Tax saved when a 30%-slab husband gifts ₹5L → wife's FD earns ₹35,000 → clubbed back to husband → ₹10,500 tax paid anyway. The saving was always an illusion. (Illustration only.)

slides.label: TAX PLANNING
slides.image_prompt: A bright orange boomerang frozen mid-arc above a neat stack of Indian rupee notes on a dark wooden surface, dramatic overhead studio lighting, vivid colour contrast, clean minimalist composition, no text or numerals visible
slides.headline: The income always finds its way back.
slides.caption: Section 64 — the clubbing rule — makes absolutely sure of it.

slides.label: LEGAL BUT GENIUS
slides.title: What actually works (legally)
points: Gift to your adult child (18+) — income taxed in their hands, not yours
Spouse draws a genuine salary from your business — documented & reasonable
Wife's own inherited money or gifts from her relatives — no clubbing applies
HUF — creates a separate legal taxpayer entity for family assets
Her own PPF / NPS contributions from her own earnings — clean split
